TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @misc{Wiki_szy_suleda, title = suleda, year = 2025, month = May, day = 5, note = {In Wikipedia [Sakizaya]}, url = {https://szy.wikipedia.org/wiki/suleda} }

S.FORM: suleda ťõ™ sapuelac mahetik namakay tapuku ku suleda. na i ladem sa, anu sienaw, 0 tu ku śļęŚļ¶ saan, yadah ku adidi'ay a nanum malitemuh, maka tu mala suleda. nasulitan nu Hulam atu Amilika Wikipayke Snow refers to forms of ice crystals that precipitate from the atmosphere and undergo changes on the Earth's surface. It pertains to frozen crystalline water throughout its life cycle, starting when, under suitable conditions, the ice crystals form in the atmosphere, increase to millimeter size, precipitate and accumulate on surfaces, then metamorphose in place, and ultimately melt, slide or sublimate away. Snowstorms organize and develop by feeding on sources of atmospheric moisture and cold air. Snowflakes nucleate around particles in the atmosphere by attracting supercooled water droplets, which freeze in hexagonal-shaped crystals. Snowflakes take on a variety of shapes, basic among these are platelets, needles, columns and rime. As snow accumulates into a snowpack, it may blow into drifts. Over time, accumulated snow metamorphoses, by sintering, sublimation and freeze-thaw. Where the climate is cold enough for year-to-year accumulation, a glacier may form. Otherwise, snow typically melts seasonally, causing runoff into streams and rivers and recharging groundwater.
S.PHON: sur~…ĺ…ôda * sapu…ôr~…ĺa ¶ mańß…ôtik namakaj tapuku ku sur~…ĺ…ôda. na i r~…ĺad…ôm sa, anu si…ônaw, * tu ku ** saan, jadańß ku adidi Ē~ °aj a nanum mar~…ĺit…ômuńß, maka tu mar~…ĺa sur~…ĺ…ôda. nasur~…ĺitan nu ńßur~…ĺam atu amir~…ĺika wikipajk…ô snow r…ôf…ôrs to forms of i ¶…ô ¶rjstar~…ĺs tńßat pr…ô ¶ipitat…ô from tńß…ô atmospńß…ôr…ô and und…ôrgo ¶ńßaŇč…ôs on tńß…ô …ôartńß Ē~ °s surfa ¶…ô. it p…ôrtains to froz…ôn ¶rjstar~…ĺr~…ĺin…ô wat…ôr tńßrougńßout its r~…ĺif…ô ¶j ¶r~…ĺ…ô, startiŇč wńß…ôn, und…ôr suitabr~…ĺ…ô ¶onditions, tńß…ô i ¶…ô ¶rjstar~…ĺs form in tńß…ô atmospńß…ôr…ô, in ¶r…ôas…ô to mir~…ĺr~…ĺim…ôt…ôr siz…ô, pr…ô ¶ipitat…ô and a ¶ ¶umur~…ĺat…ô on surfa ¶…ôs, tńß…ôn m…ôtamorpńßos…ô in pr~…ĺa ¶…ô, and ur~…ĺtimat…ôr~…ĺj m…ôr~…ĺt, sr~…ĺid…ô or subr~…ĺimat…ô awaj. snowstorms organiz…ô and d…ô*…ôr~…ĺop bj f…ô…ôdiŇč on sour ¶…ôs of atmospńß…ôri ¶ moistur…ô and ¶or~…ĺd air. snowfr~…ĺak…ôs nu ¶r~…ĺ…ôat…ô around parti ¶r~…ĺ…ôs in tńß…ô atmospńß…ôr…ô bj attra ¶tiŇč sup…ôr ¶oor~…ĺ…ôd wat…ôr dropr~…ĺ…ôts, wńßi ¶ńß fr…ô…ôz…ô in ńß…ô*agonar~…ĺ-sńßap…ôd ¶rjstar~…ĺs. snowfr~…ĺak…ôs tak…ô on a *ari…ôtj of sńßap…ôs, basi ¶ amoŇč tńß…ôs…ô ar…ô pr~…ĺat…ôr~…ĺ…ôts, n…ô…ôdr~…ĺ…ôs, ¶or~…ĺumns and rim…ô. as snow a ¶ ¶umur~…ĺat…ôs into a snowpa ¶k, it maj br~…ĺow into drifts. o*…ôr tim…ô, a ¶ ¶umur~…ĺat…ôd snow m…ôtamorpńßos…ôs, bj sint…ôriŇč, subr~…ĺimation and fr…ô…ôz…ô-tńßaw. wńß…ôr…ô tńß…ô ¶r~…ĺimat…ô is ¶or~…ĺd …ônougńß for j…ôar-to-j…ôar a ¶ ¶umur~…ĺation, a gr~…ĺa ¶i…ôr maj form. otńß…ôrwis…ô, snow tjpi ¶ar~…ĺr~…ĺj m…ôr~…ĺts s…ôasonar~…ĺr~…ĺj, ¶ausiŇč runoff into str…ôams and ri*…ôrs and r…ô ¶ńßargiŇč groundwat…ôr.
S.FORM: suleda ťõ™ sapuelac mahetik namakay tapuku ku suleda. na i ladem sa, anu sienaw, 0 tu ku śļęŚļ¶ saan, yadah ku adidi'ay a nanum malitemuh, maka tu mala suleda. nasulitan nu Hulam atu Amilika Wikipayke Snow refers to forms of ice crystals that precipitate from the atmosphere and undergo changes on the Earth's surface. It pertains to frozen crystalline water throughout its life cycle, starting when, under suitable conditions, the ice crystals form in the atmosphere, increase to millimeter size, precipitate and accumulate on surfaces, then metamorphose in place, and ultimately melt, slide or sublimate away. Snowstorms organize and develop by feeding on sources of atmospheric moisture and cold air. Snowflakes nucleate around particles in the atmosphere by attracting supercooled water droplets, which freeze in hexagonal-shaped crystals. Snowflakes take on a variety of shapes, basic among these are platelets, needles, columns and rime. As snow accumulates into a snowpack, it may blow into drifts. Over time, accumulated snow metamorphoses, by sintering, sublimation and freeze-thaw. Where the climate is cold enough for year-to-year accumulation, a glacier may form. Otherwise, snow typically melts seasonally, causing runoff into streams and rivers and recharging groundwater.
